title: Reading: If I Had Known (Hypotheticals)
learningGoal: Read and analyze a dialogue dense with 'Agar... Toh...' structures, identifying whether they are real future conditions or unreal past regrets.
instructions: Read the conversation between two friends discussing a missed train. Identify the different types of conditionals used. Notice how they drop the word 'Agar' in spoken Hindi.
readingText:
रवि: अगर हमने जल्दी की होती, तो हम ट्रेन पकड़ लेते।
अमित: हाँ, लेकिन बारिश हो रही थी। अगर बारिश नहीं होती, तो हम जल्दी निकलते।
रवि: अब क्या करें? अगर हम अगली ट्रेन का इंतज़ार करेंगे, तो रात हो जाएगी।
अमित: एक काम करते हैं। अगर तुम्हारे पास पैसे हैं, तो टैक्सी ले लें?
रवि: पैसे होते, तो मैं टैक्सी कर लेता! मेरे पास कैश नहीं है।
अमित: ठीक है। अगर मैं एटीएम गया, तो ट्रेन छूट जाएगी।
रवि: चलो इंतज़ार करते हैं। अगर किस्मत में होगा, तो कोई बस मिल जाएगी।
textInTargetLanguage:
रवि: अगर हमने जल्दी की होती, तो हम ट्रेन पकड़ लेते।
अमित: हाँ, लेकिन बारिश हो रही थी। अगर बारिश नहीं होती, तो हम जल्दी निकलते।
रवि: अब क्या करें? अगर हम अगली ट्रेन का इंतज़ार करेंगे, तो रात हो जाएगी।
अमित: एक काम करते हैं। अगर तुम्हारे पास पैसे हैं, तो टैक्सी ले लें?
रवि: पैसे होते, तो मैं टैक्सी कर लेता! मेरे पास कैश नहीं है।
अमित: ठीक है। अगर मैं एटीएम गया, तो ट्रेन छूट जाएगी।
रवि: चलो इंतज़ार करते हैं। अगर किस्मत में होगा, तो कोई बस मिल जाएगी।
baseLanguageSupportText:
Ravi: If we had hurried, then we would have caught the train.
Amit: Yes, but it was raining. If it was not raining, then we would have left early.
Ravi: Now what to do? If we wait for the next train, then it will become night.
Amit: Let's do one thing. If you have money, then shall we take a taxi?
Ravi: If I had money, then I would have hired a taxi! I do not have cash.
Amit: Okay. If I go to the ATM, then the train will be missed.
Ravi: Let's wait. If it is in destiny, then some bus will be found.

exampleSentence.baseTranslation: We would have caught the train.
Then we would have left early.

glossary.baseTranslation: it will become night (real future result)

exampleSentence.targetText: पैसे होते, तो मैं दे देता।
exampleSentence.transliteration: Paise hote, toh main de deta.
exampleSentence.baseTranslation: If I had money, I would give it.
I would have hired a taxi.

glossary.baseTranslation: if it is in destiny (real future condition)

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: अगर बारिश नहीं होती, तो हम जल्दी निकलते।
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: Agar baarish nahi hoti, toh hum jaldi nikalte.
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: If it were not raining, then we would have left early.

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: अगर हम इंतज़ार करेंगे, तो रात हो जाएगी।
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: Agar hum intezaar karenge, toh raat ho jayegi.
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: If we wait, then it will become night.

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: पैसे होते, तो मैं टैक्सी कर लेता!
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: Paise hote, toh main taxi kar leta!
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: If I had money, then I would have hired a taxi!

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: Look at Ravi's first line: 'Agar humne jaldi ki hoti, toh hum train pakad lete.' What type of conditional is this?
options: A past hypothetical (regret). They did not hurry, so they missed it.
A real future possibility. If they hurry now, they will catch it.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: Why does Amit say 'Agar baarish nahi HOTI' instead of 'Agar baarish nahi HAI'?
options: Because it is an imaginary/hypothetical statement about the past. Ending on 'hoti' without 'hai' indicates it's unreal.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: What happens when Ravi says 'Paise hote, toh main taxi kar leta!'?
options: He dropped the word 'Agar' at the start of the sentence, which is common in spoken Hindi.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: What tense is used in 'Agar hum intezaar karenge, toh raat ho jayegi'?
options: Future tense in both clauses, because it is a real prediction.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: How does Ravi summarize their situation at the end?
options: He leaves it to destiny (kismat). If it's in their destiny, a bus will be found.
He blames Amit for the rain.

correctAnswers.rq_1: A past hypothetical (regret). They did not hurry, so they missed it.
correctAnswers.rq_2: Because it is an imaginary/hypothetical statement about the past. Ending on 'hoti' without 'hai' indicates it's unreal.
correctAnswers.rq_3: He dropped the word 'Agar' at the start of the sentence, which is common in spoken Hindi.
correctAnswers.rq_4: Future tense in both clauses, because it is a real prediction.
correctAnswers.rq_5: He leaves it to destiny (kismat). If it's in their destiny, a bus will be found.

expectedSummaryPoints: Recognize the omitted 'Agar' in spoken contexts.

feedbackRules.onPass: Astounding! You can read and comprehend complex, nuanced Hindi hypotheticals.
feedbackRules.onFail: Pay close attention to the verb endings. If it ends in 'ga/gi/ge', it's a real future prediction. If it abruptly ends in 'ta/te/ti', it's an imaginary scenario.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort. Review question 3. In spoken Hindi, native speakers drop 'Agar' almost 50% of the time, but they NEVER drop 'Toh'.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: रवि: अगर हमने जल्दी की होती, तो हम ट्रेन पकड़ लेते। अमित: हाँ, लेकिन बारिश हो रही थी। अगर बारिश नहीं होती, तो हम जल्दी निकलते। रवि: अब क्या करें? अगर हम अगली ट्रेन का इंतज़ार करेंगे, तो रात हो जाएगी। अमित: एक काम करते हैं। अगर तुम्हारे पास पैसे हैं, तो टैक्सी ले लें? रवि: पैसे होते, तो मैं टैक्सी कर लेता! मेरे पास कैश नहीं है। अमित: ठीक है। अगर मैं एटीएम गया, तो ट्रेन छूट जाएगी। रवि: चलो इंतज़ार करते हैं। अगर किस्मत में होगा, तो कोई बस मिल जाएगी।

hintText: Look at the ending of 'Pakad lete'. It doesn't have 'hain' or 'the'. This means 'would have caught', not 'are catching' or 'caught'.
commonMistakes: Translating 'Hoti' as 'is' instead of recognizing it as the hypothetical cutoff.
